one_line_takeaway: AdaProb reformulates machine unlearning as a constrained KL-divergence optimization over output probabilities (uniform for the forget set, matched-to-original for the retain set) followed by backprop-based weight updates, empirically approaching random-guess MIA accuracy and lower KL-to-retrain divergence than SCRUB at under half its computational cost, though with an unexplained non-monotonic lambda ablation and one large outlier result on CIFAR-100.
key_finding: Reported numeric results from the paper's own tables (ALL-CNN, privacy protection task, Table 2): forget error for Retrain/Original/Finetune/NegGrad+/CF-k/EU-k/Bad-T/SCRUB/SCRUB+R/AdaProb on CIFAR-10 were 26.67/0.00/0.00/47.00/0.00/0.33/1.67/33.00/26.33/25.35 respectively, with test error 16.71/16.71/16.86/21.65/16.82/18.44/22.43/17.01/16.88/18.05; on Lacuna-10 forget error was 0.33/0.00/0.00/14.33/0.00/0.00/1.34/0.00/0.00/0.05. MIA attacker accuracy (Table 4, closer to 50% = better; ResNet/ALL-CNN, class/selective settings): AdaProb achieved 51.00/58.00 (ResNet class/selective) and 54.00/50.00 (ALL-CNN class/selective), versus Retrain's own 49.33/54.00/55.00/48.73 and SCRUB's 52.20/78.00/52.00/54.30 -- notably AdaProb beat SCRUB substantially on ResNet-selective (58.00 vs 78.00) and matched Retrain almost exactly on ALL-CNN-selective (50.00 vs 48.73). KL divergence to the retrained model (Table 1, forget set) was lower for AdaProb than SCRUB in most rows, e.g. ResNet on Lacuna-5: 1.35 vs 3.65; ResNet on CIFAR-5: 2.56 vs 3.01; with near parity on ResNet/Lacuna-10 (5.76 vs 5.88) and ALL-CNN/Lacuna-5 (2.02 vs 2.23). Computational time was reported as less than half of SCRUB's (abstract/intro claim; Figure 4 shows the timing plot but no exact numbers appear in the extracted text). The lambda ablation (Table 5, CIFAR-5/ResNet) showed retain error dropping from 0.21% (lambda=1) to 0.00% (lambda>=2) as lambda increases, but forget error rising sharply from 80.00% at lambda=1 down to 56.00%, 23.00%, then back up to 30.00% at lambda=2,3,4 respectively (non-monotonic). A CIFAR-100 one-class ablation showed SCRUB reaching 5.19% forget error / 0.00% retain error versus AdaProb's stated 0.00% retrain error / 98.25% forget error -- the paper does not analyze or explain this large forget-error discrepancy from its other results.
core_contribution: The paper (published as 'AdaProb: Efficient Machine Unlearning via Adaptive Probability', arXiv:2411.02622v3, though the KB record's stored title/abstract reflect an earlier draft version 'Pseudo-Probability Unlearning') proposes AdaProb, an approximate machine-unlearning method that (1) replaces a trained classifier's final-layer output probabilities on the forget set with a uniform pseudo-probability distribution to erase confident predictions, (2) refines these pseudo-probabilities via a convex, constrained KL-divergence optimization (row/column/element constraints on an N x K probability matrix, solved by coordinate descent with Lagrange multipliers, proven in Theorem 1 to converge to a unique global optimum under strict convexity), so that forget-set outputs are pulled toward looking like the retain-set/original distribution rather than staying artificially uniform, and (3) backpropagates the resulting target distributions through gradient descent on the full network weights to realize the forgetting.
threat_model: The paper's security-relevant angle is membership inference attacks (MIA), not an adversarial-input or systems threat model. The adversary is modeled as a binary 'attacker' classifier trained on the unlearned model's per-sample losses on forget-set and test-set examples, trying to distinguish 'was this example unlearned (i.e., was it originally trained on and then forgotten)' vs 'was it always held out'; attacker accuracy near 50% (random guessing) indicates strong privacy protection, following the MIA evaluation protocol adopted from Kurmanji et al. (2024, SCRUB). The paper motivates this by noting that naive unlearning methods that make the model perform poorly (high loss) on the forget set create a detectable behavioral signature that MIA can exploit, which is the vulnerability AdaProb is designed to close by keeping forget-set output distributions close (in KL divergence) to the original/retrained model's distribution rather than obviously different.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: the method is restricted to classification tasks and does not extend to unsupervised learning; it can encounter convergence issues during optimization despite the Theorem 1 global-optimum guarantee (which assumes feasible initial conditions and finite KL divergence); and it has not been extended to large language models (left as future work). Independent observations from the reviewed text: the lambda ablation results are non-monotonic without explanation; the CIFAR-100 one-class-unlearning ablation reports a forget error of 98.25% for AdaProb that is an outlier relative to all other reported forget-error numbers (mostly single digits to ~30%) and is not discussed or reconciled by the authors; exact wall-clock/timing numbers backing the '50% speedup' and 'less than 50% of computational time' claims are shown only in a figure, not a table, in the paper; and this is a workshop paper (3rd DATA-FM workshop @ ICLR 2026), not a full conference/journal submission, which typically implies a narrower/less rigorously reviewed evaluation.

quantitative_results: Table 2 (ALL-CNN, CIFAR-10) forget error: Retrain 26.67, AdaProb 25.35, SCRUB 33.00, SCRUB+R 26.33, NegGrad+ 47.00
Table 2 (ALL-CNN, Lacuna-10) forget error: Retrain 0.33, AdaProb 0.05, SCRUB 0.00, NegGrad+ 14.33
Table 2 (ALL-CNN, CIFAR-10) test error: AdaProb 18.05 vs Retrain 16.71, SCRUB 17.01
Table 4 MIA attacker accuracy (ResNet, selective): AdaProb 58.00 (std 0.93) vs SCRUB 78.00 (std 2.45) vs Retrain 54.00 (std 1.63)
Table 4 MIA attacker accuracy (ALL-CNN, selective): AdaProb 50.00 (std 0.40) vs Retrain 48.73 (std 0.24) vs SCRUB 54.30 (std 2.24)
Table 1 KL divergence forget-set (ResNet, Lacuna-5): AdaProb 1.35 vs SCRUB 3.65
Table 1 KL divergence forget-set (ResNet, CIFAR-5): AdaProb 2.56 vs SCRUB 3.01
Table 3 KL divergence test-set (ResNet, Lacuna-5): AdaProb 0.044 vs SCRUB 0.21
Table 5 lambda ablation (CIFAR-5, ResNet): forget error 80.00 (lambda=1) -> 56.00 (lambda=2) -> 23.00 (lambda=3) -> 30.00 (lambda=4), retain error 0.21 -> 0.00 -> 0.00 -> 0.00
CIFAR-100 one-class ablation (ResNet): SCRUB forget error 5.19 / retain error 0.00 vs AdaProb forget error 98.25 / retain error 0.00

Computational time: 'over 20% improvement in forgetting error, ... and less than 50% of the computational time' vs SOTA (abstract claim); exact seconds not present in extracted text (only Figure 4 plot)
